policy_statement: Adopt DEC regulations to reduce SF6 emissions and establish a timeline to phase out new SF6 equipment, coordinating with other U.S. Climate Alliance states.
mechanism_description: DEC should adopt regulations that reduce SF6 emissions and phase out new SF6 equipment over time, lowering high-GWP fugitive greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Industrial equipment management/technology change → ↓ SF6 leakage/venting (fugitive emissions) → ↓ high-GWP GHG emissions → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the use, maintenance, and procurement of gas-insulated equipment and other SF6-containing systems by set tighter handling practices and substituting/retiring SF6-based technologies. (2) Reduces physical leakage and intentional venting during operation, servicing, and end-of-life, and shifts to alternative insulating mediums with far lower GWP. (3) Lowers net greenhouse gas emissions (CO2e) by preventing atmospheric releases of SF6.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding high-GWP SF6 releases to the atmosphere by reducing leaks and eliminating SF6 equipment over time.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: direct reduction of fugitive SF6 emissions (a potent GHG) via management controls and technology phase-out.  
2) This is Mitigation because the primary intent and effect is lowering greenhouse gas emissions, not reducing exposure to hazards (Adaptation), reducing energy/material input for the same service (Resource Efficiency), or enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases: while alternative equipment may also affect energy use, safety, or grid reliability, those are secondary; the dominant climate effect remains avoided high-GWP fugitive emissions.  
4) The primary category applies to all policies under this mechanism because any SF6 management/phaseout policy’s core climate pathway is reduced SF6 emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., regulatory vs. incentive-based, utility vs. industrial coverage) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy is framed mainly as grid resilience/safety with only incidental SF6 reductions, categorization could be contested; however, under this canonical mechanism the dominant climate pathway is explicitly fugitive GHG mitigation.
key_indicators: “adopt regulations”; “reduce SF6 emissions”; “establish a timeline for phasing out new SF6 equipment”; “align policies”
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a “target-setting” element due to the “timeline,” but the dominant mechanism is a binding regulatory phase-out/requirement rather than a voluntary target.
